summary: Dan Shapiro argues that AI is creating 'technical deflation' — steadily driving down the cost of writing and fixing code. He extends Ward Cunningham's classic 'technical debt' metaphor, observing that because technical debt is owed in work rather than money, and the cost of work is deflating, carrying more debt becomes a rational strategy. Teams should ship prototypes faster and skip elegant abstractions, knowing they can repay with cheap AI hours later. Simultaneously, existing debt that causes friction has never been cheaper to eliminate. The post reframes conventional engineering wisdom: procrastination on code quality is now economically rational.
key_points: Technical debt is denominated in work, not money — so as AI deflates the cost of work, the real burden of existing debt shrinks over time
Teams should deliberately take on more technical debt than traditional wisdom would advise, shipping prototypes and skipping abstractions
Existing technical debt that causes active friction has never been cheaper to fix and should be addressed now
Code bankruptcy — scrapping and regenerating a codebase from specs — is becoming a viable low-cost option
The asymmetry is key: borrow expensive human hours today, repay with cheap AI hours tomorrow
money_quotes: Day over day, year over year, AI is driving down the cost of writing code. Economists have a word for what happens when stuff keeps getting cheaper: deflation.
But what is a 'trap' for a nation is a miracle for a codebase.
Technical debt is different: you don't owe money, you owe work. And the cost of work is what's deflating.
You are borrowing expensive human hours today, and you will get to pay them back with cheap AI hours tomorrow.
A refactor that took a month last year, takes a week today, and may be a few flicks of the fingers next year.
The debt you already have – the spaghetti code that is actually slowing your team down right now – has never been cheaper to fix.
In this new world, procrastination reduces the price of perfection.
key_insight: Because AI is deflating the cost of software work, technical debt becomes a wise investment — borrow expensive human hours now and repay with cheap AI hours later.
